So, step number 15, we call turbulence. Now what this means is that you're going to crank people's greed levels up through the stratosphere. You're going to make it such a tempting offer that is so great that they won't be able to sleep at night. One of the best compliments that I've ever gotten was somebody that came up to me at a seminar and said, damn you after I read your ad, I couldn't sleep until I ordered. So at four in the morning, I crawled downstairs and got on the phone. That was a compliment to me because I'd put so much into his head that even though he didn't buy right away, he went to sleep and he started thinking about it and he kept waking up in the middle of the night going, I really do want to sleep. I really want that. That was because of the turbulence that I put into his brain. So the tools of action you have are things like hand-reeing urgency, one-time offers. If you don't get this, it ain't going to happen again. Greed inducing limitation. The price goes up later. You can get this now for 10 bucks, but if you wait until tomorrow, it's going to be 99 bucks. The fear of being left out and that's called a takeaway. If you don't want it, I'll give it to someone else. I've done that a lot with a number of clients where I've actually got them to agree that they would number each product and number the people so that if someone didn't want it, we would actually give it to someone lower on the list than them. And that actually made people think, wait, I don't want somebody else to get it. So they started thinking about getting it themselves. It was a way to knock them off the fence. What I call the elbow to the ribs opportunity, get it now before it's released to the public. This is a way to let people in on opportunities they wouldn't otherwise be able to get to. Be honest and be bold, but follow through on all promises. If you say you're taking it off the market, for example, take it off the market. Be credible and believable at all times. I shouldn't have to be saying this, but I've been working for 30 years in the business world and I know that a lot of people make promises that they don't follow through on. So just get in the habit. If you're going to say something or you say you're going to do something, do it. It's perfectly reasonable in that example to reintroduce something later. Say you have a product, you introduce it, it's out there for a while. You say it's only going to be out for about 30 days, then we're going to pull it. And then pull it for 90 days a year, I don't know. Testing, for example, often requires introducing and then pulling some things. So it's like we're going to try it at this price. It's okay to do, it's called market testing. And so you can say we're doing a market test. We're not sure what the price is at. We're going to price it now very low to see if this is what people really need. But we're going to pull it after about a week because we're not sure that this is the price we're going to want to charge in a may go up later. So that's a perfectly reasonable reason to have limitations there. The more you tell, the more you sell. Be clear on the story and let your prospect in on your thinking. In other words, we have this thing, we're going to release it. But you know what? We're not sure that this is the price that we want to have. We've got people here saying it should be worth a lot more. But we want to make sure that somebody wants it at some price. So right now you can get it at a very low price. So you explain things as you go. It can be simple or involving. In other words, it can be very straightforward and very simple or it can be kind of involving. Like for example, the simple side of a headline would be the seven reasons attractive women choose other men over you and what to do about it today. This gets the turbulence going in a headline. It's very simple. It's very straightforward. Why are they choosing other men over me and what to do about it? Good God. It's a great little piece of turbulence to put into the prospect's mind. Or it can be more involved. Here's a piece of copy. You got to hurry though. Like I said, I have a package here for you, their DVDs. If we hear from you in the next 11 days, your package will be rushed to you by Federal Express Ground if you're in the States. And once you have your package, you'll have a full year to decide if you want to keep it or send it back for a full refund. No hassles. Your word is all we need. The staff has orders to treat you with total respect and honor your word at all times. This is a special privilege I'm offering only to guys like you on our hot list. And only in the order you are on that list. By getting this letter, you have earned a spot up the list. So here's a package. So there's a package here for you. If you don't want it, do nothing. And we'll take your name off the package and send it to the guy below you on the list. You probably won't get another chance at seeing what Greg has though. He is after all still the best kept secret weapon in golf, etc., etc. So that's a good example of the takeaway. So there are various ways to go about this. That's the fundamental elements of turbulence, which is something that will help you sell a lot more stuff. Okay, turbulence. I've got really one question here with maybe a couple details below that, depending on what your response is. What if I've got my product, I've got my offer, I think I know how I want to sell this thing. No such thing in my particular offer. I don't have any urgency limitations. Should I just start, should I make something up for the sake of doing it? Do I have to make something up? Well, not make it up, but you need to incorporate reasonable restrictions into your order, because that's part of direct response. That's part of what helps people make a decision. Back when I was saying shame on you, if you don't use all the tactics available to you, to be able to get this product into the hands of the person who can really use it. You know, if you have an ethical, good, good product. And one of those is to help them get over the idea that they're on the fence and they're thinking, well, I'm not going to make that decision right now. Well, why not put a limit on this? It can be 21 days. I mean, Rodale, there was no reason they had 21 days. They made a decision. Let's make it 21 days. Not 31 days, not 11 days, 21 days. That's reasonable, it makes sense. They actually made it a part of their marketing plan. And so they just made that down. That is our policy. Our policy is to give you 21 days. We feel that 21 days is plenty of time, blah, blah, blah. You can make it a year. We felt that a three months was a pretty good period of time. But you know what? I'm going to give you six months. No, scratch that. I'm going to give you a year, because I'm asking you to trust me. So I'm going to trust you to do the right thing. So you just, but you have to put the limit. These are just one of the decisions that you make as you go through life. Now don't make up things like we've only printed 10 of these and there are no more. That's not ethical. What is ethical though is that we've only printed a small number of these and we have no plans to print anymore because you may not. And you know, it could be part of the market test. Market tests happen all the time. You're out there. You don't know if it's going to sell. You know, this is how businesses decide whether to pursue products, whether to pursue them with more vigor or less vigor is how the market test goes. So there may be a short period of time when this is available at this price under these conditions. Yeah, that's another area where I think the reality is everything is only available at this price under these conditions. As once you're in this world, you don't know what's going to sell tomorrow at this price. So whether it's both legal, ethical, to just include this is a market test. That's a very real thing. But if you don't say that, it is a market test. You may realize you have to cut the price or you could double the price. We've done this all the time. You don't want to do this anymore. You know, this is only available for this much time. You and I have sat down, looked over stuff and decided, wow, we are undercharging. We've been undercharging for three years. So let's give them one more chance at this and then we're raising the price. Because we're, and we didn't, because we're testing this, but that's, we are, we were testing this idea. So I think it's just something that direct response, in my experience, works better if the offer is something that isn't there forever. Again, if you're selling Coca-Cola and your product is at the physical location on, in planet Earth, where someone wants to acquire a beverage of that type, that they're going to go there, your thing is going to be at eye level or the end cap, the best spot. That's a different game. However, for most of the rest of us who aren't playing that game, something that creates the nature of the offer should include a degree of urgency, limitation. If you don't do it now, the next guy is, you're going to pay more tomorrow. It's just something to really, you will probably do both better for your own business and thus better for the people you can help if you give them a reason to buy it today and not just think about it and maybe come back around someday. Well said. Hey, on that note, we'll let John explain the exercise. Again, you start a little more writing, you're going to be able to put directly to work in your ad coming up. This is another, this is a fun one. It's an interesting one. You got to be a little creative here, but creative following the template that we've laid out for you. Okay, let's talk about the exercise that I want you to do for turbulence. Just take one of these four elements, hand-reeling urgency, greed, inducing limitations, the fear of being left out or an opportunity, an able to the ribs opportunity. Get it now before it's released to the public. Whichever one of these is most appropriate for what you're selling, for what your service or product is, then just write out a couple of sentences employing one of these tactics or two of these tactics if you want. This generally comes later in a sales message. It's not something that is usually upfront. It's usually farther down after you've taken care of a lot of other objections. So you've got them on the fence. They're pretty much agreeing that this is something they want and now we're starting to negotiate. We're starting to introduce the price. We're starting to let them know what you need to do now. And we're starting to finish the process of making the sale happen. So think about how to put some turbulence in there so that the person doesn't think that they can relax. They need to get on this right now. So just give it a try and feel how urgency, limitations, takeaways, or opportunities fit in with what you're doing.
